coredata.dc:title: Dual-quenching effects of methylene blue on the luminophore and co-reactant: Application for electrochemiluminescent-electrochemical ratiometric zearalenone detection
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Lijun
Liu, Xiaohong
Bi, Xiaoya
Li, Libo
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Methylene blue (MB) is a common multifunctional indicator, which can be applied as a quencher for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) analysis as well as a classical redox probe. Although it is relatively prevalent for MB to study the mechanism with Ru-based luminophores in ECL systems, there are few studies on the effects between MB and co-reactants. In this work, we proposed the first investigation of MB on the luminophore and co-reactant of the self-enhanced ECL composites (nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots on Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles, NGQDs-Ru@SiO2), respectively. The relatively narrow ECL spectrum of luminophore (Ru@SiO2) and the suitable ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum of MB led to the ECL resonance energy transfer between them, meanwhile the appropriate energy levels among them facilitated the electron transfer, resulting in a decreased ECL signal (quench mode I). Additionally, the co-reactant (NGQDs) was prone to π-π conjugation with MB due to its abundant π-electrons, which reduced the concentration of NGQDs’ intermediates and triggered a weakened ECL signal (quench mode II). Therefore, the dual-quenching effects are ingeniously integrated and designed in one ECL-electrochemical (ECL-EC) ratiometric aptasensor for zearalenone detection, for demonstrating its efficacy in enhancing the sensitivity, which is 4.8-fold higher than Ru@SiO2 alone. This innovative ratiometric aptasensor achieved a relatively wide linear range from 1.0 × 10−15 to 5.0 × 10−8 g mL−1, and obtained a low detection limit of 8.5 × 10−16 g mL−1. Our proposed dual-quenching interactions between MB and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 will open a new prospective for ECL-EC ratiometric aptasensor, which further broaden the application in sensitive and precise analysis of mycotoxins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methylene blue
Self-enhanced ECL composites
Dual-quenching effects
ECL-EC ratiometric Aptasensor
Zearalenone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the self-enhanced ECL nanoparticles (NGQDs-Ru@SiO2)
Fabrication of electrode modification and ratiometric aptasensor
Investigation of dual-quenching effects on NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 by MB
Application of the proposed ECL-EC ratiometric aptasensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074055 ), the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87 ), and Jiangsu Province and Education Ministry Cosponsored Synergistic Innovation Center of Modern Agricultural Equipment (No. XTCX2008 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) CV, (B) EIS Nyquist plots, and (C) ECL-time and EC curves of different modified electrode: a. CS/NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/GCE, b. cDNA/CS/NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/GCE, c. BSA/cDNA/CS/NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/GCE, d. ZBA/BSA/cDNA/CS/NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/GCE, e. MB/ZBA/BSA/cDNA/CS/NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/GCE, f. 0.1 ng mL−1 ZEN/MB/ZBA/BSA/cDNA/CS/NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/GCE. Condition of CV and EIS: 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. The frequency range of EIS is at 0.1 Hz to 0.1 MHz. Inset of (B) is the Randles equivalent circuit used to fit impedance spectra. Conditions of ECL and EC: 0.4–1.25 V, 100 mM PBS (pH 7.5), PMT: 700V.

(A) Quenching efficiency of Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 after adding different concentration of MB. (B) ECL spectra of Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and UV–Vis absorbance of MB. (C) Time-resolved photoluminescence decay of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 (Ru) without and with MB. (D) Possible quenching mechanism between luminophore and MB. (E) Time-resolved photoluminescence decay of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 (NGQDs) in the absence and presence of MB. (F) Possible quenching mechanism between co-reactant and MB.

Investigation of sensitivity based on ratiometric signals by different bases. (A) Logarithmic dependence of I ECL/I EC with the different concentration of ZEN: 1 fg mL−1, 10 fg mL−1, 100 fg mL−1, 1 pg mL−1, 10 pg mL−1. (B) Relationship between different bases and sensitivity.

(A) ECL and EC responses of the NGQDs-Ru@SiO2-based ratimetric aptasensor toward different concentrations of ZEN: a. Blank, b. 1 fg mL−1, c. 10 fg mL−1, d. 100 fg mL−1, e. 1 pg mL−1, f. 10 pg mL−1, g. 100 pg mL−1, h. 1 ng mL−1, i. 10 ng mL−1, j. 50 ng mL−1. (B) The calibration curves of the ECL-EC ratimetric aptasensor for ZEN detection (plot of I ECL/I EC vs. ZEN concentration. The calibration curves of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2-based ECL intensity (C) and MB-based current (D) for ZEN detection.

(A) Comparisons of the responses to 1 ng mL−1 of ZEN and 10 ng mL−1 of AFB1, FB1, OTA, Mix (AFB1-FB1-OTA), ZEN-AFB1, ZEN-FB1, ZEN-OTA, and ZEN-Mix. (B) The reproducibility (intra- and inter-assays) of the ECL-EC ratimetric aptasensor by 6 parallel measurements of 1 ng mL−1 ZEN. (C) The stability of the as-proposed ratimetric aptasensor for 7 days.
Schematic illustration of ECL-EC ratiometric aptasensor via dual-quenching effects between NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and MB for ZEN detection.

head.ce:title: Dual-quenching effects of methylene blue on the luminophore and co-reactant: Application for electrochemiluminescent-electrochemical ratiometric zearalenone detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agricultural Equipment and Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methylene blue (MB) is a common multifunctional indicator, which can be applied as a quencher for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) analysis as well as a classical redox probe. Although it is relatively prevalent for MB to study the mechanism with Ru-based luminophores in ECL systems, there are few studies on the effects between MB and co-reactants. In this work, we proposed the first investigation of MB on the luminophore and co-reactant of the self-enhanced ECL composites (nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots on Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles, NGQDs-Ru@SiO2), respectively. The relatively narrow ECL spectrum of luminophore (Ru@SiO2) and the suitable ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum of MB led to the ECL resonance energy transfer between them, meanwhile the appropriate energy levels among them facilitated the electron transfer, resulting in a decreased ECL signal (quench mode I). Additionally, the co-reactant (NGQDs) was prone to π-π conjugation with MB due to its abundant π-electrons, which reduced the concentration of NGQDs’ intermediates and triggered a weakened ECL signal (quench mode II). Therefore, the dual-quenching effects are ingeniously integrated and designed in one ECL-electrochemical (ECL-EC) ratiometric aptasensor for zearalenone detection, for demonstrating its efficacy in enhancing the sensitivity, which is 4.8-fold higher than Ru@SiO2 alone. This innovative ratiometric aptasensor achieved a relatively wide linear range from 1.0 × 10−15 to 5.0 × 10−8 g mL−1, and obtained a low detection limit of 8.5 × 10−16 g mL−1. Our proposed dual-quenching interactions between MB and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 will open a new prospective for ECL-EC ratiometric aptasensor, which further broaden the application in sensitive and precise analysis of mycotoxins.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a phenomenon of radiative charge transfer between electrochemical excited states through the redox reaction of a luminophore (Qi and Zhang, 2020; Gao et al., 2021). In particular, Ru(bpy)3 2+-based self-enhanced ECL systems have gained significant attention due to their properties with high luminous efficiency and excellent chemical stability (Zhao et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). In detail, several nanostructures, such as silica nanoparticles and metal-organic frameworks, have been devised to prepare self-enhanced ECL composites by confining luminophore and co-reactant inside, which can shorten the electron transfer distance and reduce the energy loss (Li et al., 2021; Ning et al., 2021). Thus, these self-enhanced ECL biosensors could realize sensitive detection of complicated applications, for example, zearalenone (ZEN) detection in agricultural products (Luo et al., 2019). Although the sensitivity has achieved a relatively high level, such single-signal-based ECL biosensing is still the primary method for quantitative analysis in the numerous reported cases, and its accuracy need to be further improved, especially for trace detection (Fang et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021a).

To address the above-mentioned deficiencies, ratiometric ECL biosensors with self-calibrating function have recently been developed as one of the research topics for mycotoxin detection, including dual-potential ECL (Wu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2020b), dual-wavelength ECL, and ECL-electrochemical (EC) coupling method (Zhu et al., 2019b; Lin et al., 2019). Theoretically, such ratiometric strategies can provide internal corrections that effectively overcome external disturbances. Notably, ECL-EC based ratiometric sensing method is mainly derived from two different mechanisms and relatively independent signal transduction (Xue et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2020). Both of them do not disturb each other, benefiting from the different signal conversion pathways of the multifunctional indicator, which can not only reduce the fabrication operation of sensor, but also effectively overcome external interference, resulting in excellent analytical reliability and accuracy (Zhu et al., 2019a). For example, Lin et al. introduced methylene blue (MB) as an indicator and Ru(phen)3 2+ as a recognizer to construct a ratiometric biosensor with simultaneous acquisition of ECL and EC signals, which could realize sensitive and accurate analysis of the target (Lin et al., 2018). Therefore, it is a desirable goal to develop and apply reasonable indicators, on which to construct a ratiometric strategy with high accuracy.

MB has been utilized as a conventional multifunctional indicator. As a typical redox probe, MB is commonly applied in EC detection (Li et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021b; Liu et al., 2021b). Especially, the cationic charge of MB can be inserted into the double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) via electrostatic interaction with the phosphate backbone (Zeng et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2014), thereby applying to the construction of aptasensing strategy. For example, Dong et al. proposed a tetrahedral DNA nanostructure-enabled EC aptasensor via MB absorbing into the dsDNA as a response signal for sensitive detection of fumonisin B1 (Dong et al., 2022). Furthermore, MB can be used as a quencher and has been widely utilized for the application of Ru(bpy)3 2+-based ECL system (Lu et al., 2020b). Currently, pathways of blocking the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+ by MB may generally include ECL-resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET), electron transfer or both, which benefit from that MB features not only an excellent ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum but also a suitable narrow band gap (Mo et al., 2021; Fu et al., 2019). More importantly, it was reported that MB can easily interact with π-electrons of some carbon-based quantum dots (such as carbon nanodots, graphene quantum dots) due to their π-electron-rich aromatic rings, which played an important role in dye wastewater treatment (Bu et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021a; Wang et al., 2020a). Herein, our previous synthesized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots on Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (NGQDs-Ru@SiO2) (Luo et al., 2019) were chosen as the ECL signal probe, and MB was applied as a multifunctional indicator to construct an ECL-EC ratiometric aptasensor. In fact, the obtained ECL intensity can originate from a combination effect in the presence of probes, which involves the influence between MB and luminophore (Ru@SiO2) or co-reactant (NGQDs) of ECL system. Therefore, shedding the mechanisms of MB to the luminophore and co-reactant are of significant importance.

Given these considerations, we proposed an ECL-EC ratiometric aptasensor via electroactive MB and self-enhanced ECL composites NGQDs-Ru@SiO2. For the first time, MB was discovered to express dual-quenching mechanisms on luminophore and co-reactant of the NGQDs-Ru@SiO2-based ECL system. As described in Scheme 1 , NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 could produce a significant ECL intensity due to the intramolecular reaction between Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs. Subsequently, the modified complementary DNA strands (cDNA) formed a dsDNA with ZEN binding aptamer (ZBA), which can readily absorb MB to generate a favorable EC signal. In parallel, the ECL signal of the NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 significantly decreased, which was mainly attributed to the following two quenching mechanisms: both ECL-RET and electron transfer might generate between luminophore (donor) of the self-enhanced composites and MB (acceptor) owing to their spectral overlap and suitable energy levels, which degraded the ECL signal (quench mode I); more importantly, the co-reactant NGQDs could interact with MB via π-π conjugation, resulting in a lower concentration of NGQDs’ intermediates and thereby reducing the ECL efficiency (quench mode II). After specific recognition of ZEN and ZBA, it would lead to the detachment of the ZBA and MB from the sensing interface, where the ECL signal recovered whereas the EC signal decreased. The proposed ratiometric aptasensor achieved highly sensitive and precise analysis of ZEN, indicating a significant potential for practical applications in detection.
All materials and apparatus are described in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the self-enhanced ECL nanoparticles (NGQDs-Ru@SiO2)
ce:section.ce:para: The self-enhanced ECL nanoparticles (NGQDs-Ru@SiO2) were prepared according to our previous work (Luo et al., 2019) in Supporting Information (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of electrode modification and ratiometric aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The construction process was displayed as follows: glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm) was polished sequentially with different specifical alumina powder (0.3 μm and 0.05 μm), followed by ultrasonically cleaned with ultrapure water, acetone, ethanol and ultrapure water for each 20 s. Then, 6 μL NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 sample was modified on the surface of GCE and dried at room temperature (RT). Successively, 2 μL 0.5% chitosan solution (CS) was used to prevent NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 shedding from the electrode. Then, 3 μL cDNA was covalently anchored on the modified electrode via electrostatic adsorption, and 3 μL 1% BSA was used to block the nonspecific binding sites of CS. After that, 3 μL ZEN binding aptamer (ZBA) was immobilized by the base pairing with cDNA. After removing the unloaded ZBA with buffer solution, the modified electrode was immersed in MB solution for 1 min to enable the absorption of MB make MB into the DNA double strand. Finally, the resultant ratiometric aptasensor exported different signals by recognizing the ECL signal of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and EC signal of MB. Upon adding the target ZEN, it reacted specifically with ZBA and detached from the electrode surface, while the reduced DNA double strand led to a decrease of the embedded MB, resulting in the recovery of the ECL signal and the reduction of the EC signal. Therefore, quantitative analysis of ZEN can be achieved by measuring the value of the ratio signal values of I ECL/I EC.

In this work, a classic three-electrode system, where modified GCE used as working electrode, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, was applied in PBS solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4) under RT for all measurements. Square wave voltammetry (SWV) signals were performed from −0.4 to 1.25 V, the modulation amplitude was 25 mV, and the step potential was 4 mV. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at −700 V.

Both cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. The frequency range of EIS is set from 0.1 Hz to 0.1 MHz. Unless otherwise stated, the error bars in this work indicated the standard deviation (SD) for three parallel measurements.

ZEN in maize kernels, purchased from the local supermarket (Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, China), were analyzed by the ratiometric aptasensor. First, maize kernels were crushed into maize flour using a wall breaker, and then sample processing and extraction were performed according to previously described methods (Bi et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2019). Subsequently, the supernatant solution was diluted with 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) to different concentrations (1, 10, 50, and 100 ng mL−1). For comparison, the national standard method (high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, HPLC-MS, GB 5009.209–2016, 3rd method) was performed to verify the reliability and accuracy of the ratiometric aptasensor.

The gradual manufacturing of ratiometric aptasensor was firstly monitored by CV and EIS. As shown in Fig. 1 A, a distinct pair of current peaks of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- is observed at bare GCE. After modifying NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and CS, the peak current (curve a) decreases significantly owing to their poor conductivity. Then, the peak current further reduces due to the negatively-charged cDNA (curve b) and BSA (curve c) repelling [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- away from electrode (Jia et al., 2021). Upon the modification of ZEN binding aptamer (ZBA, curve d) and incubation in MB solution (curve e), the peak current further decreases. As expected, the peak current recovers in the presence of ZEN (curve f) because of the conjugation between ZEN and ZBA which leading some aptamers and MB to release from the electrode surface. Simultaneously, the semicircular part of the EIS is related to the electron transfer resistance (Ret), whereas the linear part is related to the diffusion limitation, thus showing the restricted diffusion of the redox couple ([Fe(CN)6]3-/4-). Therefore, EIS was also used to characterize the stepwise construction of ratiometric aptasensor (Fig. 1B). Firstly, a very small semicircle diameter can be found on the bare GCE (110 Ω), illustrating the easy diffusion of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. Then, the Ret increases after the modification of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and CS (curve a, 830 Ω), cDNA (curve b, 1017 Ω), BSA (curve c, 1083 Ω), and ZBA (curve d, 1320 Ω), respectively, resulting from their poor electrical conductivity. Subsequently, Ret continues increasing to 2275 Ω after the absorption of MB (curve e). As predicted, the Ret reduces to 1854 Ω in the presence of ZEN owing to the stripping of ZBA and MB (curve f). These results indicated that both CV and EIS could confirm that the stepwise assembly process of ratiometric aptasensor for ZEN analysis was successful.

Afterward, ECL and EC detection were conducted to assess the feasibility of using I ECL and I EC as scales for ZEN quantification. Fig. 1C shows that a very strong ECL signal of 12209 a.u. at 1.07 V is achieved with CS/NGQDs-Ru@SiO2/GCE (top curve a). Then, the ECL intensity progressively decreases with the modification of cDNA (top curve b), BSA (top curve c) and ZBA (top curve d), while the corresponding EC current (bottom curve a, b, c, d) shows no signal response. After absorbing with MB, the ECL signal is significantly reduced by ca. 80.3% (top curve e) due to the quenching effect between MB and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2; meanwhile, a relatively obvious electrochemical signal could be acquired at −0.24 V (bottom curve e). Finally, the presence of ZEN causes the specific combination between ZEN and ZBA, and drags away from the electrode surface, while part of the embedded MB is also carried away, leading to ECL signal recovery and EC current drop. In detail, 0.1 ng mL−1 ZEN can recover approximately 52.6% of the ECL signal (top curve f) and reduce the EC current by 34.4% (bottom curve f). Therefore, it can reasonably be concluded that 1) the constructed ratiometric aptasensor can be utilized to effectively detect ZEN with I ECL and I EC as scales, and 2) the decrease of I ECL after absorption of MB implies that there may be some interactions between MB and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 at the sensing interface. Therefore, we conducted a detailed investigation of the interaction mechanism between MB and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 to obtain better analytical performance of the ratiometric aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of dual-quenching effects on NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 by MB
ce:section.ce:para:
First of all, the section on ECL can be divided into signal enhancement and quenching, which forms a signal “on-off” switch. Benefiting from intramolecular ECL emission via self-enhanced composites in our previous work (Luo et al., 2019), this enhancement strategy could effectively shorten the electron transfer distance and reduce the energy loss as shown in Fig. S2: Firstly, Ru(II) in NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 is electrochemically oxidized and loses one electron to Ru(III), while NGQDs lose electron to NGQDs·+ (Equation 1). Subsequently, the unstable NGQDs·+ further lose a proton into NGQDs
(Equation 2). Meanwhile, NGQDs
with strong reducing properties can transfer Ru(III) into the excited state of Ru*(II) (Equation 3). Finally, due to the instability of the excited state Ru*(II), its return to the ground state Ru(II) is accompanied by the light emission at 625 nm (Equation 4). In terms of ECL quenching, we found that the quenching efficiency by MB was significantly different between NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and Ru@SiO2 (Fig. 2 A). For instance, the ECL quenching efficiency of Ru@SiO2 alone was only 10.6% by 1 μM MB, while that of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was quenched by 24.2%. In addition, as the MB concentration increased, the quenching efficiency of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 was always higher than that of Ru@SiO2 alone. On this basis, the effects of MB on the luminophore (Ru@SiO2) and co-reactant (NGQDs), respectively, need to be evaluated to explore the possible reaction mechanisms.

UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of MB as well as the ECL emission spectra of Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 were collected (Fig. 2B). It shows that a relatively broad UV–Vis absorbance spectrum (blue curve) is acquired at 664 nm from MB. Meanwhile, it is clearly observed that both Ru@SiO2 (black curve) and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 (red curve) exhibit a maximum ECL emission at 625 nm. Therefore, the UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of MB could be well overlapped the ECL spectrum of Ru@SiO2 or NGQDs-Ru@SiO2, ensuring the occurrence of ECL-RET between the energy donor of luminophore and the energy accepter of MB. More importantly, the closer spectral overlap between NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and MB demonstrates a higher ECL-RET efficiency (71.1%) than that of Ru@SiO2 (57.5%) (Lu et al., 2020a), which is mainly attributed to the shorter electron transfer distance and lower energy loss in the self-enhanced composites. In addition, Fig. 2C displays that the calculated average fluorescence lifetime of Ru in NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 significantly decreases with the addition of MB from 957.27 to 742.11 ns (Table S1). The remarkable variation in the average lifetime of Ru indicates that the quenching mechanism of MB on NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 is a dynamic quenching process, which demonstrates that the excited Ru would interact with MB via ECL-RET, electron transfer or both. The calculated data are as follows: E HOMO = −5.47 eV and E LUMO = −2.99 eV of Ru, and E HOMO = −5.01 eV and E LUMO = −3.24 eV of MB, where the results were obtained by CV and UV–Vis in the Supporting Information (Fig. S3). Since the luminophore expresses a higher LUMO value (−2.99 eV, vacuum) than that of MB (−3.24 eV, vacuum), this provides the possibility of electron transfer from the excited state (Ru*) to MB (Fig. 2D), confirming that the electron transfer process is also feasible for the proposed quenching system. Based on the above-described data, the excellent quenching effect is the result of the combination of ECL-RET and electron transfer between luminophore in NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and MB.

Afterward, photoluminescence (PL) and UV–Vis technologies were utilized to further investigate the interaction between NGQDs and MB. As shown in Fig. S4A, the maximum emission peak of NGQDs is located at 436 nm. After adding different concentrations of MB from 0 to 50 μM, the PL intensities of NGQDs significantly decrease accompanied with blue-shift of peak position by 10 nm, which indicates that the interaction between NGQDs and MB exists at their excited or ground state (Liu et al., 2021c). More importantly, NGQDs exhibit an obvious absorbance at 334 nm (Fig. S4B, curve a) which is ascribed to the π-π* conjugate structures of aromatic sp2 domains (Luo et al., 2017). MB expresses four characteristic peaks at 246, 292, 610 and 664 nm (Fig. S4B, curve b) which are attributed to π-π* conjugation of phenothiazine species, π-π* transitions in the benzene ring structure, dimer and monomer characteristic absorption peak, respectively (Yu et al., 2015). After mixing, it is clearly observed that the characteristic absorption peak of NGQDs is blue-shifted with 4 nm (Fig. S4B, curve c), which may be related to the surface interactions like π-π interaction between NGQDs and MB (Guo et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021a). In addition, to distinguish the dynamic or static quenching of NGQDs in NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 by MB, time-resolved PL spectroscopy was also applied. The lifetime of NGQDs in NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 shows little change in the presence of MB (Fig. 2E, Table S1), which demonstrates that a static quenching process occurs via π-π interaction between NGQDs and MB. On this basis, we hypothesize that such π-π reaction can not only affect the PL intensity, but also quench the ECL intensity of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 by consuming the co-reactant leading to a reduction of the NGQDs’ intermediates (Fig. 2F).

Since MB have dual-quenching effects on both luminophore and co-reactant of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2, the ratiometric aptasensors based on Ru@SiO2 and NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 were constructed for ZEN detection to evaluate the sensitivity of the novel sensing strategy. With the increase of ZEN concentration from 1 fg mL−1 to 10 pg mL−1, the corresponding calibration curves are illustrated in Fig. 3 A. The sensitivity of the NGQDs-Ru@SiO2-based ratiometric aptasensing strategy (the slope of the linear curve b, 0.423) is much higher (4.8-times) than that based on Ru@SiO2 (the slope pf linear curve a, 0.088) under the same range of ZEN (Fig. 3B). This result further indicates that the presence of MB plays dual-quenching effects on NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 to achieve the programmable sensitivity of the ratiometric aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed ECL-EC ratiometric aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantages of dual-quenching effects by MB on NGQDs-Ru@SiO2-based ratiometric aptasensor, the analytical performance for ZEN detection was investigated under the optimal conditions (Fig. S5), including ZBA concentration (1.6 μM), complementary binding time (50 min), MB concentration (15 μM) and incubation time (60 min). As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, the signal of I ECL recovers and that of I EC decreases with higher concentration of ZEN, and the calibration curve of I ECL/I EC values relative to the logarithm of ZEN concentration is shown in Fig. 4B. The corresponding linear regression equation is described by I ECL/I EC = 0.4389 logCZEN + 7.598 (R2 = 0.998) in a concentration range from 1 fg mL−1 to 50 ng mL−1. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.85 fg mL−1 (3SD/slope, which is shown in Supporting information). Meanwhile, the linear relationships between free NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 versus logCZEN, and free MB versus logCZEN are also assessed in Fig. 4C and D. Obviously, the ratiometric strategy based on the ratio of I ECL/I EC (R2 = 0.998, Fig. 4B) has a larger R2 than that based on individual I ECL (R2 = 0.977, Fig. 4C) or I EC (R2 = 0.970, Fig. 4D), indicating its good accuracy and reliability.

The analytical performances of the as-developed ratiometric aptasensor and other reported methods for ZEN detection are summarized in Table S2. Obviously, the as-developed ratiometric aptasensor exhibites a wider linear range and a relatively high sensitivity compared with other detection techniques such as ultra-high performance liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) (Arroyo-Manzanares et al., 2018) and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) (Xu et al., 2021), while its linear range is comparable to or much lower than those single methods like photoelectrochemistry (PEC) (Luo et al., 2020), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) (Mu et al., 2021), amperometry (Ji et al., 2019) and ECL (Luo et al., 2019), illustrating its superb analytical performance. In terms of LOD, taking advantage of the dual-quenching strategy, the ratiometric aptasensor allows the lowest LOD comparing with ratiometric fluorescence (FL) (Bi et al., 2021), ratiometric ECL (Fang et al., 2020), and ratiometric fluorescence-enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (FL-ELISA) (Liu et al., 2021d). Based on the above comparison, it can be concluded sensibly that the ratiometric detection has a better analytical performance in this sensing system compared to previous reported methods for ZEN analysis. More importantly, by the simple replacement of the cDNA and aptamer of ZEN with those of other mycotoxins, the ratiometric aptasensor can be immediately used to detect new targets, which demonstrates the generality of the developed method.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed ratiometric aptasensor, interference experiments were performed on some common mycotoxins, including aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), fumonisin B1 (FB1), ochratoxin A (OTA), and their mixture. As shown in Fig. 5 A, ZEN (1 ng mL−1) was substituted by other mycotoxins with ten times higher concentration (10 ng mL−1). Owing to the high specificity between ZEN and ZBA, the ratio value of the ratiometric aptasenosr obviously enhanced in the presence of ZEN, while that of other interferents were almost the same as blank. In addition, when the target ZEN (1 ng mL−1) was mixed with other mycotoxins (10 ng mL−1), the response illustrated no significant difference from that of pure ZEN, indicating the excellent selectivity of the proposed ratiometric aptasensor for ZEN detection.

Furthermore, the I ECL/I EC responses from six electrodes under the same modification (named as intra-assay) and single electrodes under six repeated scans (named as inter-assay) in the presence of ZEN (1 ng mL−1) were measured (Fig. 5B). The intra-assay shows a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.5% compared to 4.0% inter-assay, demonstrating the ratiometric aptasensor has fabulous reproducibility. Subsequently, the long-term stability is also evaluated utilizing the aptasensor stored at 4 °C for definite days to measure 1 ng mL−1 ZEN (Fig. 5C). After 7 days, such ratiometric aptasensor ratains 92.7% of its initial response, representing its good stability.

To further investigate the feasibility of the as-proposed ratiometric aptasensor, ZEN in maize flour, which was obtained by crushing maize kernels, was estimated by the standard addition method. To evaluate its reliability and accuracy, the national standard method for ZEN detection, HPLC-MS (GB 5009.209–2016, 3rd method), was also carried out to quantify ZEN in these samples. As shown in Table S3, the recoveries are between 93.2% and 102.8%, and the average recovery of the four added levels was 98.18%. Simultaneously, the accuracy was also evaluated using triplicate experiments, with an RSD of 1.64%–4.62%. As expected, the above recovery results are in good agreement with that of HPLC-MS method, manifesting the high reliability and accuracy of the ratiometric aptasensor effectively quantifying ZEN concentration in real sample analysis. More importantly, it is easily found that the ratiometric aptasensor is capable of detecting ZEN as low as 1 × 10−9 g mL−1, while the HPLC-MS method can only measure ZEN to 1 × 10−8 g mL−1. The above results indicate that the sensitivity of the developed aptasensor is higher than that of the HPLC-MS method, further implying that ratiometric aptasensor has great potential for sensitive detection and effective monitoring of ZEN in maize samples.

In summary, based on the dual-quenching effects between NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and MB, a highly sensitive and accurate ratiometric aptasensor was successfully constructed for ZEN detection by the ratios of ECL signal and EC current, which exhibited the following features: (i) NGQDs-Ru@SiO2, as a carrier of the ECL signal, created a relatively large initial signal. (ii) MB, by embedding into dsDNA, acted as a multifunctional indicator. On the one hand, it generated a sensitive EC current. On the other hand, MB expressed dual-quenching effects on luminophore and co-reactant of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2, which triggered a significant reduction of the ECL signal. Also, the detailed dual-quenching effects between them were investigated by ECL spectrum, ultraviolet absorption and PL spectra. (iii) The large difference between the oxidation potentials of NGQDs-Ru@SiO2 and MB produced two separate signals (including ECL and EC current) simultaneously, while avoiding potential cross-interference. On this basis, the as-proposed ECL-EC ratiometric aptasensor exhibited a low detection limit of 0.85 fg mL−1 with high sensitivity and excellent accuracy. Furthermore, the ratiometric aptasensor has been applied to detect ZEN in maize samples, and satisfactory recoveries were obtained, while its reliability was verified by the HPLC-MS method. Such a novel method could be extended to detect different mycotoxins in agricultural products.

Lijun Luo: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaohong Liu: Investigation, Resources, Software. Xiaoya Bi: Resources, Software. Libo Li: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Tianyan You: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074055), the Innovation/Entrepreneurship Program of Jiangsu Province and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018-87), and Jiangsu Province and Education Ministry Cosponsored Synergistic Innovation Center of Modern Agricultural Equipment (No. XTCX2008).
